4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The headlines all say the same thing: AI is coming for junior developers first. That much is true. But the reason usually offered — that AI can now do the simple work juniors used to do — is only half the story, and arguably the less important half.
The number that actually matters
Entry-level tech hiring has genuinely contracted, and not by a small margin. One tracking firm counted over 148,000 entry-level tech job cuts by early June 2026 alone, a pace nearly 50% faster than the year before. A separate industry analysis found actual junior hiring dropping sharply even as job postings labeled “entry-level” kept rising — which tells you something important on its own: companies are still advertising for juniors, they’re just filling those roles with people who aren’t junior anymore.
But the more careful research complicates the doom narrative in an interesting way. A large-scale study covering 62 million workers found that generative AI adoption reduces junior developer employment by roughly 9–10% over about a year and a half — real, but nowhere near the more dramatic collapse numbers getting passed around. Some of what’s being blamed on AI is ordinary cost-cutting wearing an AI-shaped costume.
